entry.title: Organic Chemistry: The Beginning
entry.content:
## Preface
I should mention this at first. Honestly speaking, I am not good at organic chemistry at all. In 2019, I took organic chemistry class in both spring and fall semester. However, knowledge was very volatile: a few years later, I can barely remember what I learned. While I am taking a year off the university, I decided to review undergraduate-level stuffs that I have learned. Therefore, my posts will be concentrated on the reactions I want (or need) to review.
I assumed that readers already know freshmen-level of chemistry and are familiar with bond-line formulas. Moreover, Chapter 1 and 2 will be skipped, and we will start from Chapter 3.
## Courses I took
Organic Chemistry 1 (Spring 2019, SNU)
Organic Chemistry 2 (Fall 2019, SNU)
## Textbook
Vollhardt, P. K.; Schore, N. *Organic Chemistry*, 8th ed.; W. H. Freeman and Company: New York, NY, **2018**.

entry.title: Thermodynamics: Introduction
entry.content:
Preface

Thermodynamics is a composite word of thermo (~heat) and dynamics (~flow). Thermodynamics is based on macroscopic, empirical laws which have nothing to do with microscopic details. Actually, it is kind of weird to put “dynamics” in thermodynamics, because usually the term thermodynamics describes equilibrium states - they do not evolve along the time - which are not dynamic. At first, we learn the law of thermodynamics and its consequences. After that, we will consider connection with microscopic, molecular level phenomenons.

Related courses I took

Physical Chemistry 1 (Spring 2018, SNU)

Thermal & Statistical Physics (Fall 2020, SNU)

Textbook
